document.title: Case Study Book on International Transaction

pages.summary: Cover page of the Case Study Book on International Transaction; it contains no substantive legal discussion.
No substantive content.

Lists the Chief Justice's message and Chapters 1-4. The listed subjects include parties and the legal framework for international contract disputes; governing law and conflict-of-laws rules; contract formation, interpretation, representations, warranties, covenants, conditions precedent, termination, force majeure, hardship, indemnification, damages, time bars, assignment, confidentiality, notices, waiver, severability, entire-agreement clauses, amendments, and language; and the opening topics on international sales contracts.

Lists the remaining sales-contract topics and Chapters 5-10. Subjects include INCOTERMS; distribution agreements; employment contracts; consumer protection law; Myanmar investment law, permits, endorsements, incentives, restrictions, and land use; government-investor relations and international investment law; and the foundations of Myanmar company law.

Lists Chapters 11-14. Subjects include company-law litigation and the courts' role; joint ventures, governance, finance, deadlock, transfer restrictions, and termination; mergers and acquisitions; and the outline and principal methods of resolving international trade disputes.

Lists Chapters 15-21. Subjects include international jurisdiction and parallel foreign proceedings; international arbitration; recognition and enforcement of international arbitral awards and foreign judgments; injunctions; international service; and evidence-related matters.

Chief Justice Htun Htun Oo explains that Myanmar's increasing openness and foreign commercial activity will generate more commercial disputes, requiring judges to understand business law, international transactions, foreign investment, corporate matters, and commercial dispute resolution. The book addresses international and local commercial law, international dispute-resolution practice, the role of Myanmar courts, and case studies presenting new legal issues. He acknowledges the Supreme Court's Business and Commercial Laws Working Group, JICA, Japan's Ministry of Justice, and Nishimura & Asahi for their contributions; the message is dated 22 March 2019.
No substantive content.

pages.section_title: Parties to Contracts for International Transactions
pages.summary: International contracts may involve private persons or entities, governments and their agencies or subdivisions, and international organizations. Contract interpretation should not favor a governmental or international organization merely because of its status, and interested non-parties have no contractual right to interfere. Case 1-a concerns a Country X seller terminating a refrigerator-equipment sales contract after its Myanmar buyer loses a required business license; the buyer asks a Myanmar court to declare the termination invalid.

In Case 1-a, the licensing authority and Myanmar court may have public or adjudicative interests, but they are not contracting parties and cannot exercise the parties' contractual rights. Case 1-b concerns a Myanmar subsidiary of a Country X company leasing land from a Myanmar company under a clause permitting termination if nationalization prevents its business. After nationalization, the subsidiary terminates and the lessor brings SIAC arbitration; the government's interest in the nationalization remains distinct from the lease dispute.

The legality of nationalization under international law or an investment treaty is separate from the private lease dispute in Case 1-b, so Myanmar cannot intervene as if it were a contracting party. In Case 1-c, however, the Myanmar government itself leases land to the foreign investor's Myanmar subsidiary under a clause allowing termination and refund of prepaid rent if nationalization prevents business, regardless of the nationalization's legality. The subsidiary invokes only that contractual remedy; because it does not challenge the nationalization, the Myanmar court may decide the refund claim but has no basis to rule on whether the nationalization violated the investment treaty.

pages.section_title: Basic framework for resolving international contract disputes
pages.summary: The chapter's first rule is that a party's governmental or international status does not alter ordinary contract analysis, and non-party stakeholders cannot interfere in the contracting parties' dispute. International contract disputes must then separate three questions: the contract's governing law, the meaning and execution of the contract, and the agreed dispute-resolution body and method. Party choice of law generally governs the contract, but mandatory Myanmar rules may still regulate matters such as formation and operation of a Myanmar joint-venture company and minimum employee rights.

A governing-law clause in a contract with the Myanmar government does not decide whether conduct also violates an applicable bilateral or multilateral investment treaty. International contracts are commonly detailed because parties have different cultures and business practices; courts should understand and respect their terms and execution process even when unfamiliar in Myanmar, subject to the governing law. Courts should likewise respect valid agreements selecting litigation or arbitration, while keeping choice of law distinct from the forum and method of dispute resolution. Government disputes also require separating contractual breach from breach of international law and examining any investment treaty.

The parties' agreement on governing law should generally be respected, but public international law remains a separate body of law. Courts need to understand the substance and execution of international contracts without rejecting them merely because they differ from Myanmar practice. A valid agreement on the dispute-resolution forum and method should be respected as far as possible and must not be confused with the choice of governing law.

Case 2-a asks whether Myanmar or Country X law governs a Myanmar buyer's termination and damages claim for defective refrigerators purchased from a Country X seller. The law directly regulating the parties' civil relationship is the governing or applicable law. Private international law, or conflict of laws, instead supplies the rules used to select that governing law and does not itself decide the contractual merits. National conflict rules differ; EU Member States use the unified Rome I Regulation for contractual obligations.

pages.section_title: Myanmar approach and classification of obligations
pages.summary: Myanmar has no statutory private-international-law code, so the book proposes internationally accepted approaches based on Rome I. The governing law regulates the civil relationship, whereas conflict rules only select it. A court must classify each issue: parties may generally choose the law governing contractual obligations, while tort, property, corporate, and other non-contractual matters follow their own conflict rules. Some issues cannot be controlled by contractual choice; for example, although parties may choose the law of a share-sale contract, the law governing the company may control the legal procedure for transferring its shares.

A single transaction may be governed by different laws for different issues: the chosen contract law governs the terms of a share purchase, while the law of the company's country governs the statutory share-transfer process. Contractual obligations include interpretation, performance, remedies and damages for breach, discharge, limitation periods, and consequences of invalidity. Rome I Article 12 assigns those issues to the applicable contract law, but directs attention to the law of the place of performance for the manner of performance and measures responding to defective performance.

pages.section_title: Party autonomy and express choice of law
pages.summary: Under party autonomy, the parties' chosen law governs contractual obligations and gives international transactions predictability. Rome I Article 3 permits an express or clearly demonstrated choice for all or part of a contract, and a Myanmar court should generally adopt that choice. The court should first inspect the contract for an express governing-law clause, while recognizing that a separate dispute-resolution clause serves a different purpose.

pages.section_title: Implied choice, third-country law, and absence of choice
pages.summary: Selecting Myanmar courts does not by itself select Myanmar substantive law. Even without an express clause, a court should investigate whether the parties separately agreed on governing law rather than automatically applying Myanmar law. Parties may choose the law of either party's country or a neutral third country; commercial reasons may include neutrality, similarity to Myanmar's common-law system, international reliability, or geographic acceptability. If no oral or written choice exists, the book recommends using international conflict-of-laws standards because Myanmar lacks a statutory code.

pages.section_title: Applicable law where the parties made no choice
pages.summary: Rome I Article 4 generally assigns sales to the seller's habitual residence, services to the service provider's, immovable-property rights and tenancies to the property's location, short private tenancies to the qualifying landlord's residence, franchises to the franchisee's residence, distributions to the distributor's residence, and auctions to the auction location. Unlisted or mixed contracts follow the habitual residence of the party making the characteristic performance. A manifestly closer connection displaces those default links; if the law still cannot be identified, the closest-connected-country test applies.

pages.section_title: Most closely connected country and Rome I Article 4
pages.summary: When Article 4's category and characteristic-performance rules cannot determine the law, the contract is governed by the country with which it is most closely connected. Internet contracting makes the physical signing place less significant; courts should assess the connection case by case. The page reproduces Rome I Article 4, including its category rules, characteristic-performance rule, manifestly-closer-connection exception, and residual closest-connection rule.

For Case 2-a, an agreed law controls; without agreement, Country X law applies because the seller is habitually resident there. In the franchise example, party choice controls, but absent choice Country Y law applies because the franchisee resides there. For conflict purposes, a company's habitual residence is its central administration, a businessperson's is the principal place of business, and a contract made or performed through a branch is linked to that branch's location.

pages.section_title: Habitual residence and limits on party autonomy
pages.summary: Rome I Article 19 fixes habitual residence at contract formation: central administration for entities, principal place of business for a businessperson, or the responsible branch or establishment where applicable. Party autonomy is subject to mandatory protections serving public or social policy. The principal exceptions identified are non-derogable consumer protection, employee protection, and overriding mandatory provisions. Case 2-b asks whether a Country Y choice-of-law term can deprive a Country X consumer of local protection for a defective smartphone bought from a Country X seller.

A consumer contract may choose foreign law, but that choice cannot remove non-derogable protections supplied by the law of the consumer's habitual residence. Thus, Country X protections remain available in Case 2-b if Country Y law would deprive the consumer of them; examples include a mandatory cancellation period and invalidation of a clause waiving cancellation rights after trader default. The page states the rule from Rome I Article 6 and begins Case 2-c concerning employee overtime despite a foreign-law clause.

Case 2-c concerns a Country X employee required to work 50 hours under a Country Y-law contract, while Country X mandates overtime beyond 42 hours. Because employees commonly have weaker knowledge and bargaining power, party choice cannot remove mandatory protections under the law that would apply absent choice. Country X's non-derogable overtime rule therefore applies if it is the employee-protective law of the habitual workplace. Rome I Article 8 generally links an unchosen employment contract to the country in or from which the employee habitually works, without treating temporary foreign work as changing that country.

Overriding mandatory laws apply for crucial public-policy reasons regardless of the parties' chosen law. The Myanmar Transfer of Immovable Property Restriction Law is used as an example: parties cannot evade restrictions on foreign ownership of Myanmar immovable property by choosing foreign law. Rome I Article 9 preserves the forum's overriding mandatory provisions, but the category should be interpreted restrictively because its boundaries are uncertain. The chapter reiterates the three principal limits on party autonomy: consumer, employee, and overriding mandatory rules.

Non-contractual obligations follow separate domestic conflict rules; EU Member States use Rome II. Case 2-d concerns a Country X resident injured and suffering vehicle damage in Country Y by an employee of a Country Z company. The general rule applies Country Y law because direct injury and property damage occurred there, regardless of where later repair or medical costs were paid. If both injured and liable parties were habitually resident in Country X, Rome II Article 4(2) would instead apply Country X law.

pages.section_title: Choice of law for torts and property classification
pages.summary: Rome II Article 4 applies the law of the place of direct damage, subject to a common-habitual-residence rule and a manifestly-closer-connection exception. Parties may expressly choose the law for a non-contractual obligation after the harmful event; commercial parties may also negotiate that choice beforehand, provided third-party rights are not harmed. Property is classified as immovable or movable, with movable property divided into tangible and intangible forms. Creation, transfer, and loss of property rights generally follow the law of the place where the property is situated.

Rights in immovable and tangible movable property are governed by the law of the property's location, promoting predictability and respecting each country's property regime; a foreign governing-law clause in a condominium sale therefore cannot displace the law of the condominium's country for the transfer of title. Intangible rights instead follow the law under which the right was created. A company's internal organization, meeting requirements, directors, and shareholder rights follow the law of incorporation, even when a joint-venture agreement selects another country's law.

The chapter concludes that tort obligations generally follow the country where direct damage occurs; creation, transfer, and loss of rights in immovable and tangible movable property follow the property's location; intangible-property rights follow the law under which they were created; and a company's internal organization follows the law of its country of incorporation.

pages.section_title: Types and negotiation of international contracts
pages.summary: International business uses varied agreements, including manufacturing, technology licensing, joint ventures, and share purchases. Even an international sale requires detailed allocation of transport, title-transfer, transit-damage, payment, and sometimes resale obligations. Negotiation commonly proceeds from identifying the parties and business concept through an LOI or MOU, detailed drafting, execution, and performance; organizational approvals and risk analysis can make this process last weeks or months.

International negotiations may legitimately end before an MOU, LOI, or definitive contract is signed. Because parties assume that risk, withdrawal ordinarily does not support a reputational or tort claim and should not itself place the withdrawing party at a legal disadvantage. Subject to legal restrictions, parties may choose their contract language; once they knowingly execute it, a party cannot avoid validity merely by claiming not to understand that language.

English is commonly used between parties with different native languages. If English and Myanmar versions are signed, courts should honor the clause specifying which controls upon inconsistency. An MOU, LOI, or term sheet records basic preliminary terms while parties negotiate a definitive agreement; legal effect depends on content, not title. Such documents commonly address transaction basics, timetable, non-binding status, confidentiality, and exclusivity. The page begins a case where a Myanmar party seeks damages after a expressly non-binding joint-venture LOI fails.

The damages claim based on failure to conclude the joint venture fails because the LOI expressly imposed no duty to sign a definitive contract. Courts should honor that commercial design, since making every preliminary understanding binding would obstruct staged negotiations. Each party ordinarily bears its own negotiation costs even without an express clause. An MOU or LOI may nevertheless be partly binding: specifically identified clauses, commonly confidentiality and exclusivity, remain enforceable while commercial terms do not.

Confidentiality is commonly a binding exception in an otherwise non-binding MOU or LOI and may instead be documented in an NDA. Disclosure needed for due diligence does not obligate either party to complete the transaction, create a right to payment for the information, or generally restrict the recipient's future business, but agreed limits on use and disclosure remain enforceable. Parties may also impose a binding exclusivity period, commonly three to six months, barring parallel negotiations that could impair the proposed transaction.

pages.section_title: Remedies and purposes of written contracts
pages.summary: A proven breach of a binding confidentiality or exclusivity clause supports the remedies specified in the preliminary agreement; even without an express remedy, provable financial loss caused by breach should be compensated. Written contracts clarify complex rights and duties, expose unresolved issues during drafting, reduce misunderstandings, and prevent disputes. They also create objective evidence of agreement. Courts interpret the text to determine rights and remedies objectively, not by a party's undisclosed subjective intention.

The literal approach derives party intent from the document as a whole and gives words their plain meaning, protecting certainty and reflecting careful negotiation. External circumstances may be considered when language is unclear or its literal result plainly contradicts business common sense, but only restrictively and against an objectively verified commercial standard. The parol evidence rule excludes earlier or contemporaneous extrinsic agreements that add to, vary, or contradict a writing intended as complete.

Case 3-a applies the parol evidence rule to exclude an oral promise that one tenant alone could sell soft drinks because it contradicts or supplements the definitive written lease. The rule encourages parties to place all essential terms in the final document. Where Myanmar lacks a protective statute, such as special legislation favoring sales agents, courts should not manufacture that policy by rewriting a freely negotiated contract; creating such protection is a legislative matter.

pages.section_title: Interpretation conclusions and standard contract structure
pages.summary: Written language normally controls; negotiation history and trade practice enter only in exceptional cases such as ambiguity or an outcome plainly inconsistent with business common sense. Courts should exclude pre-contractual evidence that changes a complete writing and, where no statute intervenes, respect party intent. A standard international contract contains a title, date and identified parties, recitals explaining background, operative provisions, a closing, and signatures by authorized representatives, with witnesses only where appropriate.

pages.section_title: Witnesses, definitions, and representations and warranties
pages.summary: International contracts do not customarily require witnesses, and Myanmar contract law does not require a witness for validity unless another applicable law does. Courts should not invalidate or discount a contract solely for lacking witness signatures unless its governing law makes them essential. Definitions establish consistent meanings for capitalized terms. Representations and warranties encourage accurate disclosure, allocate risk, and support separately specified remedies such as compensation or termination if false.

Conditions precedent must be satisfied before a party becomes obliged to close or perform and are especially detailed in transactions that are difficult to reverse. Indemnity clauses allocate risk and may cover affiliates, officers, employees, third-party liabilities, losses, legal costs, and contract breaches. A term clause defines duration and renewal; continuing relationships such as distribution, licensing, and joint ventures often use automatic renewal unless timely notice of non-renewal is given.

Termination clauses identify events, such as an uncured breach after notice, that permit early termination. Standard boilerplate commonly includes confidentiality, governing law, and dispute resolution. Confidentiality restricts third-party disclosure; governing law identifies the substantive law; dispute-resolution clauses select litigation, arbitration, or mediation and may specify institution, seat, tribunal size, rules, and language. The SIAC model shown selects Singapore-seated, English-language arbitration by one arbitrator.

pages.section_title: Assignment, force majeure, entire agreement, and amendment
pages.summary: An assignment clause prevents transfer of rights or duties to an unwanted third party without consent. Force majeure excuses delay or non-performance caused, for the agreed period and extent, by specified events beyond reasonable control. An entire-agreement clause makes the definitive contract and its schedules the complete bargain, superseding earlier oral or written understandings consistently with the parol evidence rule. An amendment clause requires later changes to be written and signed by authorized representatives.

A written-amendment clause excludes later oral modifications. A notice clause prescribes valid communication methods and addresses. A no-waiver clause protects a party from losing future enforcement merely because it did not previously exercise a right, countering a possible estoppel argument. Severability removes an invalid or unenforceable provision while preserving the remainder of the contract where possible.

A language clause identifies the legally controlling version of the contract. Courts should respect that selection; the sample provides that only the English text is binding and translations have no legal effect.

pages.section_title: Sales obligations, delivery, and risk of loss
pages.summary: A seller's core duty is to deliver the agreed goods and a buyer's is to pay the agreed price, supplemented by negotiated terms on delivery, payment, and warranty. International transport creates a period during which goods may be lost or damaged, so contracts should identify when risk passes. If loss occurs after the agreed transfer point, the buyer remains obliged to pay even without full receipt; the example transfers risk when loading aboard the vessel is completed.

ICC Incoterms standardize delivery responsibilities and risk allocation. Under both FOB and CIF, delivery and risk transfer when goods are on board; under FOB the buyer arranges carriage and insurance, while under CIF the seller does so. The parties choose terms based on cost and desired control. Because international delivery prevents simple simultaneous exchange, payment after receipt exposes the seller to buyer non-payment; a bank-issued letter of credit can reduce that risk.

At the buyer's request, its issuing bank issues an L/C naming the seller as beneficiary and promising payment against conforming documents. The seller ships, presents documents to an advising bank, and receives payment less commission; the advising bank forwards the documents and obtains reimbursement from the issuing bank. The buyer reimburses the issuing bank and receives the documents needed to claim the goods. Because banks examine documents rather than goods, the sales contract must precisely identify required documents and obligate the buyer to procure the L/C by a deadline.

Warranty disputes arise when delivered quality or specifications differ from expectations. Governing-law rules such as Myanmar's Sale of Goods Act apply unless the parties validly establish tailored warranty standards and remedies, which may replace default law. Agreed remedies may include replacement and damages. To avoid indefinite seller exposure, contracts commonly require prompt buyer inspection and notice within a fixed period; failure to give timely notice bars a warranty claim.

A distributor buys goods from the foreign seller and resells them in its own name, creating direct contracts with local users. An agent instead arranges transactions on the seller's behalf for commission, does not buy the goods, and has no sales contract with users; the foreign principal contracts directly with them. Both develop the seller's local market, but the chapter focuses on the more common distribution agreement.

Exclusivity may prevent appointment of competing distributors and, if expressly agreed, direct sales by the seller in a territory. It can lower the seller's channel costs and motivate the distributor, but limits channels and may be exchanged for non-compete and minimum-purchase duties. Exclusivity is negotiated, not presumed: absent an express term, the arrangement is non-exclusive. The word 'exclusive' alone does not necessarily bar the seller's own direct sales; that restriction should be stated expressly.

pages.section_title: Appointment and scope of distribution rights
pages.summary: Separate clauses should identify the distributor's appointment, exclusivity against other distributors or agents, and any prohibition on the seller's direct sales. The contract should define goods and geographic territory, restrict out-of-territory solicitation or orders, and address the seller's power to modify product categories during a long relationship. Because sales use the seller's marks, the agreement should expressly grant and limit the distributor's trademark license.

pages.section_title: Trademark control, non-compete, minimum purchase, and promotion
pages.summary: A distributor receives only the trademark rights expressly granted because brand names are valuable seller property. In exchange for exclusivity, the distributor may be barred from manufacturing, representing, importing, or selling competing goods during and sometimes after the contract to protect confidentiality and know-how. A minimum-purchase guarantee protects the seller from an ineffective exclusive channel, with shortfall potentially supporting termination or damages. Promotion clauses may require best efforts, compliance with seller policy, and prior approval of advertising, stores, design, and other goods.

pages.section_title: Brand standards, duration, and post-termination obligations
pages.summary: Detailed marketing controls protect consistent brand value across distributors. Distribution agreements commonly set a fixed term and require express written renewal, while disclaiming implied renewal from continued orders or sales. On expiry or termination, the trademark license ends and the distributor must stop use and return or destroy branded promotional materials. A compensation-exclusion clause may deny recovery for anticipated profits, expenditure, investment, or commitments arising from non-renewal or termination.

A fixed term allows the seller to replace a distributor that does not achieve expected market results. After termination, the distributor must cease trademark use and return or destroy branded materials. Unless the agreement provides otherwise, the distributor bears its own sales expenses and cannot demand reimbursement from the seller. Distribution agreements should expressly state these consequences in their termination provisions.

pages.section_title: Employment contracts and mandatory labor rules
pages.summary: Myanmar's Employment and Skills Development Law requires an employment contract after hiring and lists 21 subjects, including job, probation, pay, workplace, duration, hours, leave, overtime, benefits, internal rules, training, retirement, dismissal, termination, amendment, and miscellaneous duties. Party autonomy determines details, but employee-protective mandatory law overrides inconsistent agreement. The minimum-wage example states that parties cannot validly agree below the statutory floor, identified in the text as 4,800 kyat per day in May 2018.

Not every restrictive or model term is mandatory. The Ministry's model employment contract may be modified or replaced by an agreed form so long as statutory labor rights are not infringed. Matters not governed by mandatory law remain open to agreement. Administrative authorities may impose subordinate rules only when legislation authorizes them; without that authority they cannot restrict freely negotiated terms, reflecting rule-of-law and separation-of-powers principles.

pages.section_title: Statutory limits on principal employment terms
pages.summary: The text compares contractual freedom with mandatory statutes. Contract term has no stated legal limit, but pay cannot fall below minimum-wage rules; wage timing and deductions must comply with the Payment of Wages Law; overtime cannot be paid below statutory rates; and agreed hours cannot exceed the Shops and Establishments Act limit of 8 hours daily and 48 weekly or the Factories Act limit of 8 daily and 44 weekly. The Factories Act overtime rate is described as twice the ordinary rate.

pages.section_title: Holidays, leave, discipline, and labor-dispute initiation
pages.summary: Employees cannot agree away the statutory weekly rest day or receive less leave than the Leave and Holidays Act provides. Dismissal and discipline may be agreed where law is silent, but wage reductions remain limited by the Payment of Wages Act. Earned leave carries over only if agreed, while casual and medical leave do not. Labor disputes first proceed through a workplace coordinating committee or employer, then a state or regional conciliation body, which classifies them as individual rights disputes or collective disputes affecting employment relations or social peace.

Civil or criminal court proceedings remain available even during statutory conciliation or arbitration. An unresolved individual dispute may proceed from conciliation to a competent court. An unresolved collective dispute goes to a dispute-settlement arbitration body; a dissatisfied party ordinarily may appeal to the Arbitration Council or lawfully strike or lock out, while essential-service disputes must be appealed. Arbitration Council awards take effect when issued. Courts should verify completion of legally required preliminary steps before hearing a labor complaint.

pages.section_title: Consumer protection principles and prohibited conduct
pages.summary: Party autonomy applies to consumer contracts but is restricted because consumers generally cannot negotiate standard seller terms. Myanmar's Consumer Protection Law defines protected consumers as persons acquiring goods or services for personal, non-trading needs and entrepreneurs broadly as commercial manufacturers, distributors, sellers, service providers, importers, and advertisers. It establishes rights and duties, labeling and quality rules, and administrative remedies including warning, consumer relief, sales prohibition, and recall. It prohibits misleading discount, quality, standard, and product claims.

pages.section_title: Further prohibited consumer practices and limits of protection
pages.summary: The law also prohibits advertised special offers unavailable during the stated period, supposedly free goods or services that require payment, sales methods causing physical or mental annoyance, and deceptive advertising concerning quality, quantity, ingredients, use, price, service rate or timing, or delivery. Violations may attract criminal penalties and significantly restrict party autonomy. Nevertheless, a decision against an entrepreneur still requires a legal basis under consumer-protection law; the mere filing of a consumer claim is insufficient.

Foreign investment is regulated through restrictions on business type, immovable-property use, and Myanmar Companies Law requirements such as overseas-corporation registration. The Myanmar Investment Law governs both foreign and citizen investment and distinguishes prohibited activities, Union-only activities, ministry-approved activities, MIC-permit activities, parliamentary-approval activities, foreigner-prohibited activities, and citizen-joint-venture requirements. Activities not specifically prohibited or restricted by the MIL, its Rules, or an MIC notification are generally allowed.

Myanmar Investment Rules 13 and 19 confirm that an unlisted activity is not prohibited or restricted, subject to other law. MIC endorsement is not permission to conduct ordinary business; it is needed only for privileges such as long-term land leases and tax benefits. MIL Article 47 prohibits discrimination between citizen and foreign investors and discrimination by investor nationality. Cases 8-a and 8-b test denial of an unrestricted business license to a foreign-owned Myanmar company or to an investor from one foreign country while granting comparable applicants.

Nationality-based denial of the license in Cases 8-a or 8-b violates the MIL. MIL Article 52 permits nationalization only for public interest, without discrimination, through due process, and with prompt, fair, adequate compensation. Case 8-c's uncompensated cancellation of land rights and seizure of a foreign subsidiary's factory and equipment fails those conditions. Case 8-d treats exclusion of a foreign investor from management of its government joint venture as possible substantial expropriation despite no formal share seizure, particularly where no reason, prior process, or compensation is provided.

pages.section_title: Transfer of investment funds and land-use restriction
pages.summary: MIL Article 56 permits overseas transfer of regulated capital, investment proceeds and income, sale or liquidation proceeds, contractual and loan payments, dispute settlements, expropriation compensation, and lawful foreign experts' earnings; Article 63 permits inbound investment capital, expenses, and foreign loans. The Transfer of Immovable Property Restriction Law generally bars foreigners and foreigner-owned companies from acquiring Myanmar immovable property or leases longer than one year. Under its majority-control definition, a 51% foreign-owned company is covered, while a company with 51% Myanmar ownership is not.

pages.section_title: Company definitions, long leases, and investment-dispute classification
pages.summary: The TIPRL's majority-based 'foreigner-owned company' differs from the Myanmar Companies Law's more-than-35%-foreign-ownership definition, and MCL Article 464 preserves the TIPRL's separate operation. A covered company may obtain a lease exceeding one year through an MIL endorsement plus MIC permission or under the Special Economic Zone Law. Investment disputes must be classified as investor-versus-Union or investor-versus-investor: official refusal or rights interference falls in the former, while disagreement over management of a joint venture falls in the latter.

The Union's presence does not alone determine dispute type. A ministry acting as a joint investor is treated as an investor, while the same ministry refusing a regulatory permission acts in its official Union capacity. Classification therefore depends on substance, the nature of the dispute, and the government's role. All parties must first attempt resolution under MIL Article 83. For specified official errors or frustration of statutory rights, the investor gives written notice to the MIC Investment Assistance Committee, follows any statutory amicable process, and may then proceed to court or arbitration if unresolved.

Investor-to-investor disputes follow the contract's dispute-resolution mechanism; absent one, competent courts or arbitral tribunals act under applicable law. A Myanmar-law/Myanmar-court clause leads to Myanmar adjudication under Myanmar law; a valid arbitration clause requires the court to decline or dismiss the merits. A Myanmar court may apply chosen foreign substantive law while retaining Myanmar procedural law. If forum is unspecified, jurisdiction follows Myanmar law; if governing law is also unspecified, Myanmar private-international-law principles select it.

pages.section_title: International investment law and IIA coverage
pages.summary: International investment law chiefly operates through international investment agreements, especially bilateral investment treaties. IIAs protect qualifying investors and investments and commonly let an investor claim against the host state in court or arbitration. Their principal provisions define protected investors and assets, prescribe treatment, and establish dispute resolution. The Japan-Myanmar example defines an investor as a qualifying national or enterprise investing in the other party's territory and begins a broad asset-based definition of investment.

Protected investment may include enterprises, shares, debt, contractual rights, financial claims, intellectual property, statutory or contractual rights, and tangible or intangible property. Thus, a Country X parent's Myanmar subsidiary shares and factory may both be protected. Standard IIA treatment includes national treatment, most-favored-nation treatment, fair and equitable treatment, limits and compensation for expropriation, and an umbrella clause covering other host-state obligations.

National treatment requires a host state to treat a treaty partner's investors and covered investments no less favorably than comparable domestic investors throughout establishment, operation, and disposition. Country Y therefore breaches the IIA in Case 9-a by legislating less favorable treatment for a Country X investor than domestic competitors. Most-favored-nation treatment similarly requires treatment no less favorable than that granted in like circumstances to investors from third countries.

pages.section_title: MFN application and fair and equitable treatment
pages.summary: In Case 9-b, a Country X investor may plausibly invoke the MFN clause to obtain treatment Country Y later granted more favorably to Country Z investors. Fair and equitable treatment is a broad and inconsistently defined protection, sometimes specified to prohibit denial of justice and require due process, full protection, and security. Its application is case-specific and may protect an investor's legitimate, fair, and reasonable expectations.

pages.section_title: Legitimate expectations and conditions for expropriation
pages.summary: A clarified FET clause may require due process and reasonable security without making every breach of another treaty provision an FET breach. In Case 9-c, twenty years of license renewals plus the state's relocation assurance create a reasonable expectation of continued licensing until a new location is provided; unexplained non-renewal without investor misconduct violates FET. Expropriation under modern IIAs is generally lawful only if non-discriminatory, for public interest, pursuant to due process, and accompanied by compensation.

A host state must provide prompt, adequate, and effective compensation for lawful expropriation. The model clause values the investment immediately before expropriation or its public anticipation, includes commercial interest until payment, and requires prompt, realizable, transferable compensation. In Case 9-d, Country Y violates the IIA by relying on domestic special law to expropriate a Country X investor's factory for public purpose without financial compensation.

pages.section_title: Umbrella clauses and investor-state dispute resolution
pages.summary: An umbrella clause converts a host state's breach of an investment-related contractual or authorization obligation into a possible treaty breach. Without such a clause, breach of a joint-venture agreement by a government acting as investor remains an investor-to-investor contract dispute. In Case 9-e, refusal to deliver state land promised by lease, solely for higher rent from another party, breaches both lease and IIA where the umbrella clause applies. IIAs commonly authorize investor-state arbitration and may require a final choice between court and arbitration.

pages.section_title: Finality and mechanisms of investment arbitration
pages.summary: Investment arbitration commonly uses three arbitrators and yields a final, binding award without ordinary court appeal. Courts must respect the treaty parties' consent and the investor's election, rejecting duplicate state litigation unless the investor agrees. The model provision requires attempted negotiation, permits a claim after 180 days, and offers host-state courts, ICSID, ICSID Additional Facility, UNCITRAL, or another agreed institution. Treaty consent and the investor's submission satisfy relevant written-consent requirements under ICSID and the New York Convention.

Once an investor submits the dispute to the host state's courts or one of the treaty-authorized arbitration mechanisms, that procedural choice is final under the quoted Korea-Myanmar investment agreement.

The Myanmar Companies Law defines a company as a body corporate incorporated and registered under the law, including a private company limited by shares with no more than 50 non-employee members and a public company with any number. A body corporate is legally independent from shareholders: their respective creditors cannot attach each other's property. It is a legal person able to contract and bear rights and duties, and its continuing relationships with shareholders, directors, employees, suppliers, and customers are organized by statutory governance rules.

Companies limited by shares allow many investors to pool funds for continuous, large-scale business. Limited liability confines a shareholder's exposure for company debt to unpaid amounts on subscribed shares. Because shareholder assets ordinarily cannot satisfy company debt, creditors must assess and preserve the company's own asset base, which is their primary repayment source.

In Case 10-a, material suppliers to an underfunded joint-venture subsidiary cannot recover the subsidiary's debt from its two shareholders beyond their subscribed capital, unless a shareholder separately guaranteed or assumed that liability. Creditors should investigate company finances before contracting. The MCL protects the company asset pool by restricting returns of capital to shareholders and by giving creditors priority over shareholders in voluntary winding up.

A share buy-back or capital reduction requires post-transaction solvency, fairness to shareholders as a whole, no material prejudice to creditor payment, shareholder approval, and required procedure. Dividends likewise require solvency, overall shareholder fairness, and no material creditor prejudice. In winding up, company property first satisfies liabilities and preferential payments pari passu; only residual assets are distributed to members according to their rights.

pages.section_title: Shareholder decisions and separation of management
pages.summary: Shareholders decide only fundamental matters assigned by law or constitution, including constitutional amendment, capital reduction, director appointment or removal, member benefits, and voluntary winding up, generally by voting rights attached to shares. Day-to-day management belongs to the board or sole director under MCL Article 160. This separation of ownership and management lets shareholders appoint professional managers, although family companies in Myanmar often combine the two roles.

Widely held listed companies illustrate practical separation of thousands of owners from a smaller board. Because directors control management, MCL Articles 165-172 impose fiduciary duties: reasonable care and diligence; good faith in the company's best interests and proper purpose; no improper use of position or information for advantage or company detriment; and compliance with law and constitution. The page begins the statutory rule against misuse of information.

pages.section_title: Further director duties and business judgment rule
pages.summary: Directors must avoid improper information use, legal or constitutional breach, reckless trading that risks serious creditor loss, obligations the company cannot reasonably perform, and undisclosed material personal interests. Because risk is inherent in management, an unsuccessful decision does not itself breach duty. The business judgment rule protects an informed, loyal decision made in good faith for proper purpose, without material personal interest, and rationally believed to serve the company.

pages.section_title: Limits of business judgment and functions of shares
pages.summary: Business-judgment protection must be applied only after verifying all statutory conditions so shareholder interests are not unjustly harmed. Shares divide company equity into small, equal units, enabling broad fundraising. They are movable property transferable under law and the constitution, allowing an investor to recover capital through sale without winding up the company. A private company's constitution may restrict transfer to prevent ownership by an unknown or unsuitable third party.

pages.section_title: Private-company limits and rights attached to shares
pages.summary: A private company is limited to 50 non-employee members, cannot invite public subscription, and may constitutionally restrict share transfers. Unless validly varied, a share carries one vote on a poll, an equal dividend right, and an equal residual-asset right. Those management and financial rights remain legally limited, and shareholders do not directly own company property.

The MCL assigns courts roles in exercising statutory rights, supervising procedures, and imposing penalties. A derivative action lets a shareholder sue in the company's name on a company claim, usually when directors fail to pursue a colleague's fiduciary breach. This mechanism addresses board conflicts while remaining subject to court permission and statutory safeguards.

In Case 11-a, a 30% foreign shareholder may seek leave to sue a director who caused the joint venture to overpay his separate company after the controlled board refused action. Under MCL Article 197(b), the court grants leave if company action is improbable, the applicant acts in good faith, suit serves the company's best interests, a serious issue exists, and at least 14 days' written notice was given or waiver is appropriate. The chapter then introduces relief against majority abuse harming minority shareholders.

Case 11-b concerns an 80% shareholder using a special resolution to facilitate forced sale of a 20% shareholder's shares. Holders of at least 10% of an affected class who did not support a variation may seek an injunction against unreasonable restriction of class rights. Under MCL Articles 192 and 194, eligible persons may also challenge company affairs, acts, or resolutions that oppose shareholders' collective interests or are oppressive, unfairly prejudicial, or discriminatory. Available orders can address winding up, constitution, governance, transfers, buy-outs, compelled acts, and compensation.

A promoter may contract for a company before it legally exists, for example leasing its future office. The incorporated company can later ratify the agreement under MCL procedures. If it does not, the counterparty ordinarily lacks a contract claim against the company but may ask the court under Article 35 to order return of transferred property, validate all or part of the contract, or grant other necessary relief. Even after ratification, the original maker may be ordered to pay damages or other just relief.

pages.section_title: Promoter liability, winding up, and procedural remedies
pages.summary: MCL Article 36 permits just and equitable relief against the person who made a ratified pre-incorporation contract, in addition to or instead of relief against the company. Shareholder-led winding up generally proceeds without court involvement, while other winding up may be court-supervised to ensure fair distribution; powers include appointing a liquidator, examining holders of company property, and arresting a likely absconding contributory. Courts may also correct statutory filing and recordkeeping defects, beginning with extensions for constitutional-amendment filings.

pages.section_title: Corrective court orders and company-name penalties
pages.summary: Courts may rectify registers, convene improperly omitted meetings, compel access to accounts and statutory registers, appoint enough directors for quorum, correct or extend registration of charges, compel inspection of charge instruments and debenture registers, and order production to the registrar. MCL Article 144 fines a company and knowing officers 50,000 kyat for non-publication of the company name. A signer may also become personally liable on an improperly named obligation unless the counterparty understood it was the company's or liability would be unjust or inequitable.

A knowing and willful officer involved in a statutory default may receive an additional dishonesty-based penalty and, on the registrar's application, director or officer disqualification. A person responsible for a defective prospectus may avoid liability by proving lack of knowledge, honest factual mistake, immateriality, or circumstances the court considers reasonably excusable. Certain omitted disclosures require proof that the person knew the undisclosed matter.

The quoted MCL provision confirms that a court may excuse immaterial or otherwise reasonably excusable prospectus non-compliance after considering all circumstances. For the specifically referenced disclosure requirement, a director or responsible person is liable for omission only if knowledge of the undisclosed matter is proved.

pages.section_title: Nature and formation of joint ventures
pages.summary: A joint venture is a collaborative investment by two or more partners establishing and operating a company, although parties may use other alliances such as outsourcing. Formation commonly occurs either by partners creating a new jointly owned company or by one partner buying part of an existing company from another. The page illustrates a new company owned 51% and 49% by two partners.

A joint venture may be formed when one partner acquires shares in the other's target company. It can involve more than two partners and any combination of Myanmar and overseas corporations. A domestic-foreign venture can combine the overseas partner's finance, technology, and brand with the domestic partner's real estate, regulatory access, workforce, and local business network.

The joint-venture or shareholders' agreement is the principal document defining partner rights and duties; its terms are usually reflected in the company constitution and supported by licenses for technology or brand, leases, and related contracts. Share ratios normally correspond to investment value. For businesses that MIL Article 42(c) permits only as a citizen joint venture, MIR Article 22 generally requires at least 20% direct Myanmar-citizen ownership, limiting contractual freedom over the ratio.

Myanmar company-law voting thresholds make ownership percentage central: more than 50% controls ordinary resolutions and at least 75% controls special resolutions, though control should correspond to contributed value. A 50:50 venture risks paralysis because neither party can decide without the other and is therefore discouraged. Joint-venture terms are negotiated case by case but typically specify company identity, office, business, capital, ownership, funding schedule, and detailed governance.

The agreement regulates shareholder meetings and board meetings, including convocation, notice, timing, subject matter, quorum, voting, written resolutions, and remote attendance. It allocates each partner's power to appoint and remove directors. It also defines managing-director authority and whether multiple managing directors may act separately or only jointly. Financial statements are examined by an auditor; foreign partners often seek an internationally reputable audit firm or local affiliate. The page introduces minority veto rights.

Reserved-matter vetoes let a minority partner consent to major shareholder or board decisions despite ordinary majority rules. Common reserved matters include constitutional amendment, share issuance, capital change, major borrowing, guarantees or disposals, restructuring, dissolution, and liquidation. Because vetoes can create deadlock, the agreement should escalate from good-faith party negotiation to senior executives, then resolve failure through one partner's share transfer to the other or dissolution.

Additional partner funding is commonly pro rata, but inability of one partner to contribute may permit the other to fund the shortfall, alter ownership and governance, and provoke dispute. Mutual reliance justifies an initial ban on third-party share transfers without consent. Later transfers may be allowed after a stated period, subject to a right of first refusal on the same terms. Breach may trigger a put option requiring the breacher to buy the innocent party's shares at a premium or a call option requiring sale of the breacher's shares at a discount.

pages.section_title: Valuation, partner roles, non-compete, and dissolution
pages.summary: Put and call options may also resolve deadlock; agreements often assign share valuation to a named reputable financial adviser or audit firm using accepted methods. Partner roles and related technology, brand, property, employee-transfer, and loan agreements should be explicit. Non-compete duties protect the venture during operation and may continue for a reasonable stated period after termination to protect secrets and know-how, subject to local law. A venture may end by mutual agreement or expiration of necessary government approval.
The joint-venture agreement also terminates when a partner's put or call option is exercised following deadlock or breach, or when shares are purchased under the contractual right of first refusal.

pages.section_title: Meaning and principal form of M&A
pages.summary: Mergers and acquisitions involve a third party purchasing an entity's shares or assets. Outstanding shares transfer from an existing holder; new shares are issued by the target to the purchaser. The chapter focuses on a share transfer, illustrated by a seller transferring all target-company shares to a buyer. Unlike a continuing collaborative joint venture, an M&A transaction is a one-time sale and purchase.

A purchaser can enter business faster than by starting from scratch and acquire existing land, buildings, employees, supplier and customer relationships, and permits. A seller converts business value into cash, for example on a founder's retirement. Both sides require a reasonable share valuation using professional methods discussed in the joint-venture chapter.

The M&A process identifies parties, discusses cooperation, records basic terms in an LOI or MOU, performs legal, accounting, tax, and business due diligence, negotiates and signs the final agreement, and closes. Due diligence reviews documents, written answers, and interviews to test feasibility, price fairness, and obstacles to the buyer's plans. Seller participation is voluntary, and an NDA should protect sensitive disclosures from misuse.

Due-diligence findings may lawfully lead the buyer to withdraw. A standard share-purchase agreement includes recitals, definitions, the share-transfer obligation, conditions precedent, closing, representations and warranties, covenants, indemnification, and boilerplate provisions.

International trade disputes are commonly addressed by negotiation, arbitration, or litigation, with arbitration or litigation providing final resolution. Litigation may raise concerns about home-party preference, limited technical expertise, undeveloped trade law, and delay through appeals. Arbitration offers cross-border enforceability under the New York Convention, neutral and expert decision-makers, confidentiality, and speed through award finality. Myanmar court involvement depends on international jurisdiction and any agreed dispute-resolution method.

Myanmar courts also handle international service of judicial documents and may be asked to recognize and enforce judgments of foreign courts or awards of foreign arbitral tribunals, subjects developed in Chapters 17, 18, and 20.

Case 15-a concerns a Myanmar buyer suing a Country X seller in Myanmar over a defective machine. Myanmar civil jurisdiction generally follows Code of Civil Procedure Articles 9 and 15-20, but a valid agreement selecting another dispute method, such as arbitration in neutral Country Y, should prevent the court from exercising otherwise available jurisdiction. For an overseas corporate defendant to 'carry on business' in Myanmar under Article 20, the text understands registration under the Myanmar Companies Law to be necessary.

pages.section_title: Validity and effect of dispute-resolution agreements
pages.summary: Parties may select arbitration or litigation in a foreign court. Contract Act Article 28 invalidates absolute restraints on ordinary proceedings but expressly preserves arbitration; a foreign-court clause is also not an absolute restraint and is generally valid. Choice of forum must remain distinct from choice of substantive law. On a timely application made no later than the first merits statement, Myanmar Arbitration Law Article 10 requires referral and a stay unless the arbitration agreement is null, inoperative, or incapable of performance.

pages.section_title: Stay, appeal, termination, and foreign-court clause
pages.summary: No appeal lies from an order referring parties to arbitration, but refusal to refer may be appealed. After a stay, the parties should notify the court when arbitration ends; notification of the award permits termination of the suit under Code of Civil Procedure Article 114. If the parties selected a designated foreign court instead, the Myanmar court returns the complaint under Order 7 Rule 10.

pages.section_title: Myanmar arbitration framework and agreement elements
pages.summary: Myanmar is a New York Convention state and enacted the 2016 Arbitration Law on that basis. At a party's timely request, a court hearing a matter covered by a valid arbitration agreement must refer the parties and stay the suit, unless the clause is null, inoperative, or incapable of performance. An arbitration clause should state whether arbitration applies, its seat, institution and rules, number of arbitrators, and language.

The sample clause submits disputes about existence, validity, or termination to institutional arbitration and specifies seat, tribunal size, and language. Under the doctrine of separability, invalidity of the main contract does not automatically invalidate its arbitration clause. UNCITRAL Model Law Article 16 and Myanmar Arbitration Law Article 18 let the tribunal rule on its own jurisdiction and treat the clause as an independent agreement. Institutional rules then govern commencement, parties, consolidation, tribunal, proceedings, awards, and costs.

A foreign institution's arbitration rules, rather than Myanmar's full domestic procedure, govern a foreign-seated arbitration, although selected Myanmar Arbitration Law provisions still apply. Common institutions include ICC, SIAC, LCIA, and HKIAC. The administering institution and hearing location may differ, and parties may select either. Tribunals usually have one or three arbitrators; absent agreement, applicable rules determine number and appointment procedure.

The SIAC examples default to a sole arbitrator unless agreement, complexity, amount, or other circumstances justify three. For three arbitrators, each party nominates one and the SIAC President fills a party default and appoints the presiding third arbitrator absent another effective procedure. Parties may choose any language, commonly English; if they do not, the governing rules and tribunal determine it.

Case 17-a asks how a Country X buyer can enforce in Myanmar a Country Y arbitral award ordering a Myanmar seller to pay MMK 10 million for a defective machine. Because Myanmar is party to the New York Convention and its Arbitration Law applies specified provisions and Chapter 10 to foreign-seated arbitration, the award proceeds under the statutory recognition and enforcement regime.

The New York Convention requires contracting states to recognize awards as binding without substantially more onerous conditions or fees than for domestic awards. Under Myanmar Arbitration Law Section 45, the applicant submits the authenticated original or copy of the award, the original or certified arbitration agreement, and evidence that the award is foreign. Foreign-language materials require a certified English translation by an accepted diplomatic, consular, certified-translator, affidavit, or Myanmar-law method.

pages.section_title: General enforcement rule and narrow exceptions
pages.summary: Unless a statutory refusal ground applies, Section 46 requires enforcement of a foreign award as if it were a court decree. A foreign award is one made in another New York Convention member state under an arbitration agreement. Company A's MMK 10 million award should therefore be recognized and enforced. Non-arbitrability under Myanmar law and conflict with Myanmar national interest or public policy are exceptional grounds and should be applied cautiously to respect party agreement.

pages.section_title: Grounds to refuse a foreign arbitral award
pages.summary: The resisting party may prove incapacity, invalid arbitration agreement, lack of proper notice or opportunity to present its case, excess of arbitral scope, improper tribunal composition or procedure, or an award not yet binding or set aside or suspended at the seat. The court may independently refuse for Myanmar non-arbitrability or national-interest conflict. A refusal order may be appealed under Section 47(a)(3).

Case 18-a concerns enforcement in Myanmar of a Country Y judgment ordering a Myanmar seller to pay MMK 10 million for a defective machine under a foreign-court clause. If Myanmar assets must be reached, Code of Civil Procedure Section 13 makes the foreign judgment conclusive between the same parties on directly adjudicated matters unless one of six exceptions applies.

A foreign judgment is not conclusive if issued without competent jurisdiction, not on the merits, based on an incorrect view of international law or refusal to apply applicable Myanmar law, contrary to natural justice, obtained by fraud, or sustaining a claim founded on breach of Myanmar law. A certified copy creates a rebuttable presumption of foreign jurisdiction under Section 14. Myanmar courts should test only those exceptions, not retry the merits. Subject to case-specific Section 44A enforcement, the Country Y judgment can operate like a Myanmar judgment and bar re-litigation.

An injunction restrains an illegal act and may be temporary or perpetual; Myanmar temporary injunctions follow Code of Civil Procedure Order XXXIX, while perpetual injunctions follow the Specific Relief Act. Case 19-a concerns an exclusive Myanmar distributor allegedly using confidential smartphone technology to sell a competing product. A Country N tribunal issues interim relief stopping those sales pending final award, the distributor disobeys, and the foreign manufacturer seeks Myanmar enforcement.

Institutional rules such as SIAC Rule 30.1 permit tribunals to grant injunctions or other interim relief and require security. The Country N tribunal may restrain Company Y's competing sales until final award. If disobeyed, Company X must seek recognition and enforcement in Myanmar under the Arbitration Law and Chapter 17 principles. Temporary injunctions issued by foreign courts are considered under the foreign-judgment framework discussed in Chapter 18.

Lawful adjudication requires both jurisdiction and valid service of complaints, subpoenas, and related documents. The chapter presents outbound service from a Myanmar court on a Country A resident and inbound service requested through diplomatic channels by a Country A court on a Myanmar resident, assuming jurisdiction exists but no service treaty binds the states.

pages.section_title: Sovereignty and consent to service abroad
pages.summary: Service by a court is an exercise of state sovereignty and cannot freely occur in another country without consent. Consent may be given generally by multilateral or bilateral convention or individually. Myanmar is not party to the major civil-procedure and Hague service conventions identified and appears to have few bilateral service treaties. Convention-based procedure typically passes from the requesting court through supreme court, foreign ministry, embassy, receiving foreign ministry, and local service body.

Without a convention, Myanmar must obtain the receiving state's consent through diplomatic channels for each case. The requesting package may require embassy note, court request, translations, documents to serve, and translated service copies. Because completion may take about four months, hearing dates should allow roughly six months. Although Myanmar procedure mentions postal service where available and no local agent exists, direct foreign service is internationally unusual; courts should first research and comply with the receiving state's law and sovereign requirements.

pages.section_title: Responding to inbound foreign service requests
pages.summary: Myanmar should answer a no-treaty request through diplomatic channels and consider normal relations with the requesting state, availability of Burmese translations, and sufficient time for the defendant to understand and prepare. Myanmar courts ordinarily should not assess the foreign claim's merits because that belongs to the foreign court. If service is permitted, a Myanmar court should effect it by a method accepted under the Code of Civil Procedure.

Case 21-a asks whether a Country X sales contract for Country X machinery is inadmissible in Myanmar solely because it lacks a revenue stamp. Myanmar Stamp Duty Act Section 35 excludes a chargeable but unstamped instrument, but not every instrument is chargeable and foreign law may require no stamp. The Myanmar court must determine whether the particular agreement falls within Myanmar stamp duty rather than automatically rejecting its validity or admissibility.

Under Stamp Duty Act Section 3(c), a foreign-executed scheduled instrument may be chargeable when it concerns Myanmar property or an act to be done in Myanmar and is received in Myanmar. Case 21-a therefore requires findings on place of subject matter and performance and receipt of the original. Foreign-language evidence should be accompanied by an official Myanmar translation, and the court should receive proof of accuracy under the Court Manual.

pages.section_title: Translation certification and foreign evidence requests
pages.summary: The submitting party should practically prepare the Myanmar translation. Accuracy may be supported by a translator's notarized affidavit; foreign notarization should be legalized by the Myanmar consul. Evidence-taking abroad is an exercise of sovereignty requiring foreign consent and cooperation. Myanmar is not party to the identified Hague evidence conventions, so each case requires bilateral engagement and compliance with the foreign state's law. Case 21-c concerns a Country X court asking a Myanmar court to examine a Myanmar bus driver in a civil travel-injury action.

pages.section_title: Myanmar execution of a foreign evidence commission
pages.summary: Under Code of Civil Procedure Order XXVI Rule 27, the High Court may issue a commission when a foreign court seeks evidence in a civil proceeding from a witness resident within its appellate jurisdiction. Those facts may be shown by the designated consular certificate or a foreign letter of request transmitted officially or produced by a party. Under Rules 28-29, the High Court may commission the local court where the witness resides, or another suitable person within its original jurisdiction, to conduct the examination.

pages.section_title: Myanmar request to take evidence abroad
pages.summary: Case 21-d reverses the situation: a Myanmar court needs testimony from a driver in Country X for a civil travel-injury claim. Section 77 and Order XXVI Rule 5 permit a commission or letter of request for a necessary witness outside Myanmar, transmitted through diplomatic channels. The request should identify the Myanmar civil proceeding, witness's foreign residence, necessity of the evidence, and concrete questions, while also satisfying the receiving state's procedural requirements.

The annex begins Regulation (EC) No 593/2008 on law applicable to contractual obligations (Rome I). Recitals 1-7 link uniform conflict rules to EU judicial cooperation, predictable outcomes, legal certainty, judgment circulation, and consistency with Brussels I and Rome II. Recitals 8-10 explain family, negotiable-instrument, and pre-contractual exclusions. Recitals 11-13 make party choice a cornerstone, treat an exclusive-jurisdiction agreement as evidence of choice, and permit incorporation of non-state rules or conventions by reference.

Recitals preserve mandatory law where a chosen law is foreign to an otherwise domestic or intra-EU situation and balance foreseeability with a closest-connection escape clause. In absence of choice, specified contract categories control, otherwise characteristic performance and ultimately closest connection apply; linked contracts may inform that connection. Carriage concepts are clarified, weaker parties require favorable conflict rules, and the consumer rule depends on professional activity directed to the consumer's state and a contract concluded within that activity, not mere website accessibility.

Consumers receive non-derogable protection of their habitual-residence law when professionals operate in or direct activity to that state. The recitals classify financial services and securities terms, exclude certain immovable-property and financial-instrument matters from the general consumer rule, preserve uniform market and settlement-system law, and require special rules for carriage and insurance. Posted-worker mandatory protection remains available. The page begins the rule that individual employees cannot lose non-derogable protection through choice of law.

Employees retain non-derogable protection, and temporary foreign work does not change the habitual workplace when return is expected. Public policy and overriding mandatory rules are exceptional and narrowly construed. The recitals clarify assignment, choose a single habitual-residence criterion, coordinate Rome I with sectoral EU law and existing conventions, and invoke subsidiarity and proportionality. Ireland opted in; the United Kingdom and Denmark did not participate under the stated protocols.

Article 1 applies Rome I to contractual obligations in civil and commercial conflict cases, excluding revenue and administrative matters, status and capacity, family and succession, negotiable instruments, arbitration and jurisdiction clauses, company organization, agency authority, trusts, pre-contractual dealings, specified insurance, and generally evidence and procedure. Article 2 gives universal application to the selected law. Article 3 permits express or clearly demonstrated choice for all or part, later change without harming form or third parties, while preserving non-derogable law of an otherwise exclusively connected state or EU situation.

Article 4 assigns unchosen law by contract type: seller, service provider, property situs, qualifying landlord, franchisee, distributor, auction location, or governing multilateral-system law; mixed or unlisted contracts follow characteristic performance, subject to manifestly closer and residual closest-connection rules. Article 5 separately links goods carriage to carrier residence plus receipt, delivery, or consignor connection, otherwise agreed delivery place; passenger carriage uses passenger or carrier connections and limits permissible choices. Article 6 begins consumer contracts.

Article 6 applies the consumer's habitual-residence law when the professional operates in or directs activity there and the contract falls within it; choice cannot remove non-derogable consumer protection. It excludes specified foreign-only services, most carriage, most immovable-property contracts, financial instruments and issuance terms, and multilateral systems. Article 7 excludes reinsurance, lets parties choose law for large-risk insurance, otherwise generally uses insurer residence subject to closer connection, and limits choice for other Member-State risks to enumerated laws.

Article 7 applies special rules to compulsory insurance, may split multi-state risks, and defines risk location through insurance directives. Article 8 preserves non-derogable employee protection; absent choice, law follows the country in or from which work is habitually performed, then the engaging establishment, subject to closer connection, with temporary posting not changing workplace. Article 9 defines and preserves forum overriding mandatory rules and may recognize those at place of performance when performance is unlawful. Article 10 governs consent and material validity; Article 11 begins formal validity.

Article 11 validates contract form under substantive governing law or specified laws of place, presence, or residence, but consumer form follows consumer residence and mandatory immovable-property form follows situs. Article 12 makes applicable law govern interpretation, performance, breach and damages, discharge and limitation, and nullity, while considering place-of-performance rules. Article 13 restricts reliance on foreign incapacity. Articles 14-16 allocate law for voluntary assignment, contractual and legal subrogation, and recourse among multiple liable debtors.

Article 16 completes recourse among co-debtors; Article 17 applies the law of the target claim to unagreed set-off. Article 18 applies governing-law presumptions and burdens of proof while allowing forum-administerable proof modes accepted by forum or formal-validity law. Article 19 defines habitual residence; Articles 20-22 exclude renvoi, narrowly permit forum public-policy refusal, and handle states with multiple legal systems. Articles 23-25 coordinate Rome I with sectoral EU law, the Rome Convention, and existing international conventions.

Between Member States, Rome I prevails over exclusively inter-member conventions on covered matters; Member States must notify conventions and denunciations for Commission publication. Article 27 required reviews of insurance, consumer protection, and third-party effects and priority of assignments. Article 28 applies Rome I to contracts concluded after 17 December 2009. Article 29 sets entry into force after publication, general application from 17 December 2009, and earlier convention-list operation from 17 June 2009.

The annex begins Regulation (EC) No 864/2007 on non-contractual obligations (Rome II). Recitals ground uniform conflict rules in EU judicial cooperation, predictability, legal certainty, and free movement of judgments, coordinated with Brussels I and contractual-law instruments. The Regulation applies regardless of forum type but excludes state-authority acts, interprets family relationships under forum law, treats non-contractual obligation autonomously, includes strict liability, and makes applicable law govern capacity to incur tort liability.

Rome II combines a general direct-damage rule with special rules and a manifestly-closer-country escape. Direct personal or property damage, not indirect consequences, supplies the principal connection; common habitual residence is an exception. Product liability uses a foreseeability-sensitive cascade. Unfair competition follows the affected competitive or consumer market, competition restriction follows the affected market, and environmental damage includes adverse resource change, impaired ecological function, and reduced biological variability.

Environmental rules favor the injured party and reflect prevention and polluter-pays principles. IP infringement follows the country of claimed protection; industrial action generally follows its location. Special rules cover unjust enrichment, unauthorized management of another's affairs, and pre-contractual liability. Party choice is permitted under safeguards for weaker parties and third parties. Public policy and overriding mandatory rules remain exceptional; courts may consider actual victim losses and place-of-event safety rules even when another law governs.

The remaining recitals preserve existing international conventions, support exceptional third-country agreements, invoke subsidiarity and proportionality, note UK and Irish participation, and exclude Denmark. Article 1 applies Rome II to civil and commercial non-contractual conflicts but excludes public-authority liability, family and property regimes, negotiable instruments, corporate organization and officer or auditor liability, trusts, nuclear damage, privacy and personality rights, and generally evidence and procedure. Article 2 covers actual or likely consequences of tort, unjust enrichment, unauthorized management, and pre-contractual dealings.

Article 3 gives universal effect to the selected law. Article 4 applies the law of direct damage, except common habitual residence or manifestly closer connection. Article 5 uses a product-liability cascade of victim residence, acquisition place, and damage place where marketing occurred, subject to defendant foreseeability and closer connection. Article 6 applies affected-market law to unfair competition and competition restrictions, allows a constrained forum-law choice for multi-country competition harm, and prohibits contractual derogation.

Article 7 lets an environmental claimant choose between direct-damage law and law of the harmful event. Article 8 applies lex loci protectionis to IP and place-of-infringement law to residual unitary EU IP issues, without party derogation. Article 9 applies the place of industrial action. Articles 10-11 determine unjust enrichment and unauthorized management by linked relationship, common residence, place of enrichment or act, then closer connection. Article 12 applies actual or hypothetical contract law to pre-contractual liability, with fallback damage, common-residence, and closer-connection rules.

Article 13 preserves the IP rule for Chapter III claims. Article 14 permits post-event choice and freely negotiated pre-event choice among commercial parties, expressed with reasonable certainty and without harming third parties or non-derogable domestic or EU law. Article 15 defines the applicable law's scope across liability, defenses, damages, preventive relief, transfer, beneficiaries, vicarious liability, discharge, and limitation. Articles 16-19 preserve forum mandatory law, consider local safety rules, allow direct insurer claims where either governing law permits, and govern subrogation.

Article 20 assigns contribution among jointly liable debtors to the law governing the paying debtor's obligation. Articles 21-22 govern formal validity and proof. Article 23 defines corporate and business habitual residence, including branch connections. Articles 24-26 exclude renvoi, treat territorial legal units separately, and permit refusal only for manifest forum public-policy incompatibility. Articles 27-28 preserve sectoral EU conflict rules and existing international conventions, while Rome II prevails between Member States over conventions exclusively among them on covered matters.

Member States had to notify relevant conventions and denunciations for publication. The Commission review covered foreign-law treatment, traffic-accident conflicts, and privacy and personality-right claims. Rome II applies to damage-causing events after entry into force and generally from 11 January 2009, while the convention-list provision applied from 11 July 2008. The Regulation is binding and directly applicable in participating Member States.

The Commission committed to studies and possible measures on law applicable to privacy and personality-right violations, improved treatment of cross-border road-accident victims including insurance options, and Member-State court practices for applying foreign law in civil and commercial matters.
